author: @obsidianjane4413
text:
Its not more efficient to put the generator on the ground.  The tower strong enough to take the loads of the turbine, will be more than strong enough to support the mass of the gen.  The losses and extra mass of any transmission medium you can think of will be far higher than just wires.
Lots of very smart people have been paid a lot of money to figure out the very best/cheapest configuration.

author: @brucemarshall4087
text:
This is a very interesting mechanism, Robert. I’ve never seen it before and it certainly is an impressive concept. I am wondering about the efficiency though.

Do you have any way of measuring the actual efficiency of it? 

I’m also curious as to whether it’s practical in terms of transmitting a load. You seem to be a guy that loves to take things apart and create proof of concept, so I have a suggestion for you. 

The one tool that lends itself most thoroughly to this device is the angle grinder. They are available in every shop, and the one thing they all have in common is the need for 90° power transmission.

You have mentioned that you have a 3-D printer so you should be able to actually create a housing that would go onto an existing angle grinder in order to test this mechanism under real working load in real working conditions. It would be interesting to see. 

Your design shows three rods. Could you design and build it with five or 10 rods? Would that increase or decrease the efficiency?

author: @katiekumcgil
text: modern turbines dont actually work,they have such large starter motors that have to keep the turning 24 hrs day ,it is only when the wind is strong enough does the system let the windn take over,so most times you see them turning ,they aren running off the grid ,not to mention due to the unregularity of wind power they actually have to dump the steam in the coal fired plats when the wind gets up to balance the grid ,Insanity, not for the company that owns the turbine get like 600 hundred thousand a year for every turbine weather or not it makes poiwer at all

replies.author: @vylbird8014
replies.text:
Not true at all. Turbines generally do not have to keep spinning, and they will not spin when there isn't wind enough to run them. There are two exceptions when they will run 'in reverse' though, but only two:

- When the wind is too low to start the turbine rotating, but would be enough to generate power, some large turbines will briefly use the motor in a 'kick start' to get them going.
- In conditions of windless cold weather where ice formation could be a problem, the blades will be occasionally rotated - but not continuously.

The bit about 'dumping steam' is just complete nonsense. Coal power generation wouldn't do that except in an emergency situation. Steam is money! If there is a sudden increase in renewable generation then it would be the gas turbine power stations that reduce their output, or hydroelectric. Not coal.

author: @TheMadManPlace
text:
I wonder...
In the case of a wind turbine, if you had a hydraulic pump driven by the "propeller" and a hydraulic motor at the bottom driving the generator with hydraulic lines connecting the two, would that not be a solution to the problem of raising very heavy generator mechanisms a few hundred meters into the air?
Turbines revolve "slowly" and with everything "up there", so must the massive gearbox units be so that the generator can "get some rotational speed". If that all happens on the ground, then thats another reduction in the weight that needs to be lifted - ALSO, inspections of these parts could be done daily as opposed to once a month or whatever the schedule is now. The only 2 parts "up there" that would need to be inspected would be the propeller bearings and shaft and the hydraulic pump.
Another plus would be that if the blades fail and or the tower collapses, there would be more than a third of the cost of the whole unit safely on the ground and left undamaged once the dust settles which could be used in the next one built and save BIG costs.

replies.author: @vylbird8014
replies.text: The cheaper ones, yes. The cost of going for the cheap ones is usually in the amount of fiddling it takes to get them working right, as you have to adjust various screws to get everything lined up to a precision of about a fifth of a millimeter. As for longevity, that's where cheaper is better: All of the parts are off the shelf, so when it breaks down you can usually make a repair without any problem.

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
Any time you apply a load across a shaft, there will always be a counter torque.

Therefore, if the blades are on a swivelling head, the driving force being sent down the axis of the support will cause the head of the wind mill to counter-rotate [same way you need a counter rotor on a single direction helicopter].

The more load you develop from the wind against the top of the vertical torque assembly, and the more resistance your electromechanical power harvesting equipment loads the opposite end of the shaft, the more torsional deflection you are going to get.

When I was younger, I saw some models explaining this.

Also, it would be cheaper, simpler, stronger, and more reliable to just use 45 degree bevel gears anyhow.

What you need is a way to transfer the force of the wind, without it being dependent upon the position of the rotary head sending the force forward into the system.

This is why I said 'coaxial hydrostatic drive'.

replies.author: @vylbird8014
replies.text: I can see hot to fix the torque problem by using two shafts, counterrotating. Either coaxial, or side by side. But there comes a point where the complexity of this drivechain becomes so much that it's just more practical to just put the generator inside the nacelle and have the whole assembly follow the wind direction together.

replies.author: @vylbird8014
replies.text:
You'd get smoother operation. For reasons that will become apparent if you stare and think long enough though, it makes a difference if you have an even or odd number of rods in terms of vibration.

Annoyingly neither is ideal: Even number gives less lateral vibration, odd number gives steadier torque!

author: @chrissscottt
text:
I asked Chat GPT why wind turbines don't employ ground based generators. It replied;

"Wind turbines typically employ generators located at the top of the tower rather than ground-based generators for several reasons:

Efficiency: Ground-based generators would require the transfer of rotational energy from the top of the turbine to the ground. This would involve a long shaft to transfer power, and such mechanical connections can lead to significant energy losses due to friction and other inefficiencies. By placing the generator at the top of the tower, the rotational energy from the blades can be converted directly into electrical energy without the need for long transmission lines.

Weight and Cost: Placing the generator at the top of the tower allows for a lighter and more cost-effective design. A ground-based generator would need to be much larger and more robust to handle the same power output, which would increase the cost of construction and maintenance.

Stability and Safety: Wind turbines are subject to dynamic loads and vibrations due to the wind, especially at greater heights. Placing the generator at the top of the tower reduces the forces and stresses on the tower structure, making it more stable and safer to operate.

Maintenance and Accessibility: Having the generator at the top of the tower allows for easier access during maintenance and repairs. Technicians can use the turbine's internal staircase or lift to reach the generator compartment, simplifying the maintenance process.

Land Use: Placing the generator at the top of the tower minimizes the footprint of the wind turbine, allowing for more efficient land use and reducing potential impacts on the environment and nearby properties.

Overall, the design of wind turbines with top-mounted generators has proven to be more efficient, cost-effective, and reliable compared to ground-based generator alternatives. While there are ongoing research and development efforts in the renewable energy sector, the current standard practice remains to use top-mounted generators in wind turbine designs.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @bikerfirefarter7280
text:
Interesting mechanical coupler, but I'm curious how it holds up to high torque.
You can get 90-deg drives from spur gears; and UJs will also do it, you just use 2 in series, each turns through 45-deg. Or 2 CV joints in 45-deg series, same as joints in car front wheels. They are high torque, low loss, and good reliability/life.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the plastic model  made held up well mate - i would have torn up plastic gears spinning them in a drill like that - something most folks seem to have missed

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I get that it’s just a demo of principle. I've seen similar used to couple high-torque shafts at low angles (e.g. ship prop shafts, aligned to the engine with 5-10 deg offset) they are strong. My point was at higher angles the 'overhang' of the rods puts a lot of bending force on them. I was just curious what the limiting angle would be before a different mech was a better choice. I didn't make that clear. I wasn't criticising. Sorry if I poked a nerve.
I've seen the principle in pressure-washers and hydraulic pumps/motors. And Torpedo motors, with a sine plate driven by pistons, giving huge output in a small case. But not turned at an aggressive angle, just a straight axle. Apparently wear issues become a problem at high rpm/torque, but then torpedoes aren’t exactly expected to last.  ;-/

author: @DB-thats-me
text:
This joint has another trick. 
You show a solid 90deg elbow, but substitute a swivel elbow, you have a CV joint that has a large range of angular displacement. 
You mention this at the end of the post. Provided the transmission tubes can’t of themselves rotate (think X cross section through corresponding hole) you can get very low angles of displacement. 
There are uploads of exactly this form of constructed in lego if you want to search. 
Another great post, Cheers.

P.S.  This coupling is capable of high speed with low noise!  It was used for a while in medical applications where this was not only desirable, but crucial. 😷

author: @adymode
text: I suspect that except for when the generator is free coasting open circuit, as soon as any power is drawn, the resulting torque at the generator will jolt the turbine out of the wind, and the whole system become very unstable and energetic.

author: @RichardBetel
text:
Hobson couplers are simple to understand and make, but I’m pretty sure they’re rather inefficient: it’s a lot of rotating mass, which makes your wind turbine ineffective at low speeds, there’s lots of sliding motions which maximizes friction, the coupler bars are oscillating on 2 axes with accompanying noise, the 90 degree bar coupler thingies are going to be failure prone…

I think a simple bevel gear would be way more reliable and efficient.

replies.author: @exploreseafaring
replies.text: The mass issue was my first instict, but with alternative materials such as carbon fibre or the old chanel favorite of bamboo, and lineral bearings etc etc. things may be overcome.

replies.author: @TheChzoronzon
replies.text:
I'll quote, but not sure of the quality: "They can handle a huge amount of torque, much more than gears and about twice as much as a normal universal joint. A universal joint of the same size has half as many pins per input/output side. The long piston shaft is why they can handle torque so well; the leverage is great and spreads the weight over a large bearing surface that's usually hardened (and 15 ui finished) steel-on-steel, but expensive versions use ceramic pistons and liners and even cheap versions use steel on teflon. All of the above have excellent bearing abilities.

They can't go very fast though. It's also very large which I assume is why you don't see it used as an actual linkage, but this mechanism is extremely common in the form of bent/variable axial pumps, which make up the majority of hydraulic pumps/motors. They're used because they're cheap (and in some situations because of the size and shape, for instance they're universally used for torpedo motors).

They're also extremely efficient (up to 98% iirc), but obviously several times less efficient than gears."

Also: you have then on Amazon, for example: Cal-Van Tools 485 45 Degree 1/4" Gearless Angle Drive

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: @TheChzoronzon That's where I've seen the principle. Pressure-washers and hydraulic pumps/motors. And Torpedo motors. A sine plate driven by pistons. Huge output in a small case. But not turned at an angle, just a straight axle. Wear issues become a problem at high rpm/torque.

author: @excell211
text: You could use linear bearings on the shafts in order to reduce friction, although I don't know how well these would really work out...

replies.author: @NiteLynr
replies.text: Surprisingly well, actually. Coming from a 3d printing background, one of the first things I used to replace on my older kit-style printers (think Anet A8's) was the linear bearings. I found that Delrin ones were vastly superior, giving longevity and much improved performance. I would most certainly recommend bearings of this type to improve performance. A possible alternative would be thrust bearings but there's added cost, wear and tear with those.
